era_context: The Classical Greek world in the early 4th century BCE, in the immediate aftermath of the King's Peace of 387 BCE. The new framework for Greek interstate relations under Persian hegemony was being implemented. Sparta was consolidating its dominance of mainland Greece. Rome was continuing its expansion in central Italy. The Persian Achaemenid Empire under Artaxerxes II was at the height of its power.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Most of the Mediterranean beyond Greece and Rome

events.title: Implementation of the King's Peace in Greece
events.description: In the year following the King's Peace of 387 BCE, the terms of the peace were being implemented across Greece. Greek city-states were adjusting to the new framework of Persian-guaranteed autonomy. Sparta, as the dominant military power, was positioned to enforce the peace and maintain order in Greece. The peace established a period of relative stability in Greek interstate relations, though tensions and rivalries remained beneath the surface. This period would see Sparta's hegemony over Greece solidified, though it would eventually be challenged by Thebes under Epaminondas.
key_figures: Artaxerxes II (Persian Great King, guarantor of the peace)
events.certainty_note: The implementation of the King's Peace in the years following 387 BCE is attested in sources, though specific events in 386 BCE are not always clearly distinguished from adjacent years.
cross_references: Followed the King's Peace of 387 BCE
Led to Spartan dominance of Greece in the following decades

events.title: Plato's Academy develops philosophical curriculum
events.description: Plato's Academy, recently established (387 BCE), was developing its philosophical curriculum and attracting students from across the Greek world. The Academy focused on mathematics, dialectic, and philosophical inquiry, with the goal of training students in the pursuit of truth and virtue. Plato was developing his theory of Forms and other philosophical doctrines that would profoundly influence Western thought. The Academy was becoming a center of intellectual activity in Athens.
key_figures: Xenocrates (early student and later head of the Academy)

sources.name: Diogenes Laërtius, Lives and Opinions of Eminent Philosophers

events.certainty_note: The development of Plato's Academy in the years following its founding is attested in Plato's dialogues and later biographical sources. Specific activities in 386 BCE are not always clearly documented.
cross_references: Followed the founding of the Academy in 387 BCE
Part of the intellectual flowering of Classical Athens

events.title: Roman military campaigns in central Italy
events.description: Rome continued its military expansion in central Italy, subjugating or allying with neighboring Italic tribes and city-states. Specific campaigns in 386 BCE are poorly documented in surviving sources, but the general trajectory of Roman expansion is clear. Rome was gradually consolidating its position as the dominant power in the Italian peninsula, though this process would take several more centuries.

sources.name: Livy, History of Rome (Ab urbe condita)

events.certainty_note: The general context of Roman expansion is clear, but specific events in 386 BCE are not well-documented. Livy's account is written centuries after the fact and may conflate or misdate events.
cross_references: Part of Rome's gradual expansion in the 4th century BCE
Followed the consolidation of Roman power in Latium

events.title: Persian administration of Egypt under Artaxerxes II
events.description: Egypt remained under Persian rule, administered through Persian governors and local Egyptian officials. The Persian Achaemenid Empire maintained tight control over Egypt as a key source of grain and revenue. Egyptian administrative records and demotic papyri document the continuation of Persian rule and the functioning of the Egyptian bureaucracy under Persian oversight.

sources.name: Egyptian demotic papyri and administrative records

events.certainty_note: Persian control over Egypt is attested in Egyptian administrative records and Greek sources. The continuation of Persian rule in 386 BCE is part of the broader context of Artaxerxes II's reign.
cross_references: Part of Artaxerxes II's reign (404–358 BCE)
Related to the King's Peace of 387 BCE

disconfirming_evidence: No major disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the sparse documentation for specific events in 386 BCE reflects the broader historiographic problem of ancient history: years that are not marked by major political upheavals or military campaigns often leave minimal traces in surviving sources. The implementation of the King's Peace is attested in sources, but the specific details of how it was implemented in 386 BCE versus 387 BCE or 385 BCE are not always clear.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 386 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Xenophon's Hellenica. The year is less well-documented than 387 BCE (the year of the King's Peace) because it lacks a major political or military event that captured the attention of ancient historians. Roman history remains poorly documented and dependent on Livy's much later account. Persian sources are minimal, though Egyptian administrative records provide evidence of Persian control. The record is heavily weighted toward Greece and Rome; other regions are absent or minimally represented. This year illustrates a broader historiographic challenge: the survival of historical records is often determined by the occurrence of dramatic events rather than by the actual historical importance of a year.

graph_edges.note: The implementation of the King's Peace followed its negotiation in 387 BCE
The development of Plato's Academy followed its founding in 387 BCE
